message.content:
#set page(margin: 1in)
#set text(size: 11pt, font: "New Computer Modern")

#align(center, [
  #text(size: 1.5em, weight: "bold")[Picard-Fuchs equations and mirror maps for hypersurfaces]
  #parbreak()
  Source-backed arXiv sample
  #parbreak()
])

= Extracted Lists

The list environments below are extracted from arXiv LaTeX source and wrapped for pdfLaTeX validation.

+ If we analytically continue along a simple loop around $z=0$ in the counterclockwise direction, $t$ becomes $t+1$. (It will be convenient to also introduce $q=e^(2 pi i t)$, which remains single-valued near $z=0$.)

+ There are cycles $gamma_0$ and $gamma_1$ such that $integral.op_(gamma_0) omega(z)$ is single valued near $z=0$, and
  $t = (integral.op_(gamma_1) omega(z)) / (integral.op_(gamma_0) omega(z))$
  in an angular sector near $z=0$.

+ There is a period function for $omega(z)$,
  $f_0(z) := integral.op_(gamma_0) omega(z)$
  which is single-valued near $z=0$. This period function is unique up to multiplication by a constant. (This implies that the cycle $gamma_0$ is also unique up to a constant multiple.)

  In particular, the family of meromorphic $n$-forms
  $tilde(omega)(z) := (omega(z)) / (integral.op_(gamma_0) omega(z))$
  will have the property that
  $integral.op_(gamma) tilde(omega)(z) equiv 1$
  for some $gamma$, and it is the unique such family up to constant multiple.

+ Fixing a choice of period function $f_0(z)$ as in part (1), there is a period function
  $f_1(z) := integral.op_(gamma_1) omega(z)$
  such that $phi(z) := f_1(z) / f_0(z)$ transforms as
  $phi(z) arrow.r.bar phi(z) + 1$
  upon transport around $z=0$ in the counterclockwise direction. The ratio $phi(z)$ is unique up to the addition of a constant.
